
# Task Analysis {.appendix}

Here are some questions to guide you through the process of doing a task analysis. You don't have to specifically answer each one of these (and some may not apply), but they should get you started thinking in the right direction. 

What are the prerequisites, for both knowledge and e.g. software setup?

- Know basics of statistics
- Know and be comfortable with using R and installing different packages
- Understand what win and loss data looks like.
- You will need R and other necessary packages. Like the ones talked about in the needs.qmd

::: callout-note

Make sure you list out the packages you're assuming people are familiar with, once you've written the code. Some basics to start with might be dplyr/tidyr for data formatting.

:::

What are the basic requirements for any data that the method is used on? 
  - Are missing values allowed? 
  - Should the data be confined within a certain range? 
  - Does the data have to be approximately normally distributed?

The data set should include player 1, player 2, match result, and maybe the date of the match. Missing results should be removed before running the model. Also we don;t need normal distribution since the outcome is usually win or loss.

What are the basic components of the task?
  -Outline these in a bit more detail

1. Data preparation
   - Clean and verify competitor names
   - Ensure chronological order if using dynamic ratings
   - Encode outcomes numerically

2. Model selection
   - Choose between static models (Bradley–Terry) or dynamic models (Elo/Glicko)
   - Decide between different approaches

3. Parameter specification
   - Select starting ratings
   - Choose tuning parameters (e.g., K-factor)

4. Model estimation or updating
   - Fit model using maximum likelihood (Bradley–Terry), or
   - update ratings (Elo-style)
   
5. Evaluation
   - Assess predictive accuracy
   - Compare alternative parameter settings
   - Examine rating stability
   
6. Interpretation
   - Rank competitors
   - Interpret rating differences as win probabilities
   - Evaluate uncertainty wherever needed

What decisions does the user need to be prepared to make on the fly?

- Which ratings to use
- How sensitive the ratings should be
- How to measure if predictions are accurate

What questions should the user ask of the "first draft" of the product? What adjustments may need to be made?

- Do the rankings make sense?
- Are the ratings maybe changing too fast or too slow?
- Does it predict future models well enough?
- Some adjustments could be adjusting the paramters and running it again.

## Additional Guidance 

Your check-in should answer these basic questions (and similar concerns that apply more directly to your topic). 

Once you've completed the check-in, you can use this section to jump-start an introduction/set-up/getting started section in your user guide. 
This document should remain as an appendix to your main report.
